category.text: $course$/SDS4PH/Module 1 - Foundations of Spatial Thinking for Health

name.text: M1Q01: Tobler’s First Law of Geography states that:

questiontext.text: Tobler’s First Law of Geography states that:

generalfeedback.text: Tobler’s First Law (1970) is the foundational principle of spatial analysis. It implies that nearby locations tend to have more similar attribute values than distant locations — the basis for spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: Everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things

feedback.text: Correct! Tobler’s First Law (1970) is the foundational principle of spatial analysis. It implies that nearby locations tend to have more similar attribute values than distant locations — the basis for spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: Spatial data must always be projected before analysis

feedback.text: Incorrect. Tobler’s First Law (1970) is the foundational principle of spatial analysis. It implies that nearby locations tend to have more similar attribute values than distant locations — the basis for spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: The Earth is best represented as a flat surface

feedback.text: Incorrect. Tobler’s First Law (1970) is the foundational principle of spatial analysis. It implies that nearby locations tend to have more similar attribute values than distant locations — the basis for spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: Distance decay applies only to infectious diseases

feedback.text: Incorrect. Tobler’s First Law (1970) is the foundational principle of spatial analysis. It implies that nearby locations tend to have more similar attribute values than distant locations — the basis for spatial autocorrelation.

generalfeedback.text: Spatial autocorrelation measures whether nearby features have similar (positive SA) or dissimilar (negative SA) attribute values. Positive spatial autocorrelation means similar values cluster together.

answer.text: The presence of systematic spatial variation in a mapped variable

feedback.text: Correct! Spatial autocorrelation measures whether nearby features have similar (positive SA) or dissimilar (negative SA) attribute values. Positive spatial autocorrelation means similar values cluster together.

answer.text: The correlation between two different variables measured at the same location

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial autocorrelation measures whether nearby features have similar (positive SA) or dissimilar (negative SA) attribute values. Positive spatial autocorrelation means similar values cluster together.

answer.text: The tendency of GIS software to introduce measurement errors

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial autocorrelation measures whether nearby features have similar (positive SA) or dissimilar (negative SA) attribute values. Positive spatial autocorrelation means similar values cluster together.

answer.text: The relationship between map projection and data accuracy

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial autocorrelation measures whether nearby features have similar (positive SA) or dissimilar (negative SA) attribute values. Positive spatial autocorrelation means similar values cluster together.

name.text: M1Q03: A positive Moran’s I value close to +1 indicates:

questiontext.text: A positive Moran’s I value close to +1 indicates:

generalfeedback.text: Moran’s I ranges from -1 (perfect dispersion) through 0 (random) to +1 (perfect clustering). A value near +1 means areas with similar values are near each other — high values cluster near high values, and low near low.

answer.text: Strong spatial clustering of similar values

feedback.text: Correct! Moran’s I ranges from -1 (perfect dispersion) through 0 (random) to +1 (perfect clustering). A value near +1 means areas with similar values are near each other — high values cluster near high values, and low near low.

answer.text: Strong spatial dispersion (checkerboard pattern)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Moran’s I ranges from -1 (perfect dispersion) through 0 (random) to +1 (perfect clustering). A value near +1 means areas with similar values are near each other — high values cluster near high values, and low near low.

answer.text: Complete spatial randomness

feedback.text: Incorrect. Moran’s I ranges from -1 (perfect dispersion) through 0 (random) to +1 (perfect clustering). A value near +1 means areas with similar values are near each other — high values cluster near high values, and low near low.

answer.text: No spatial pattern in the data

feedback.text: Incorrect. Moran’s I ranges from -1 (perfect dispersion) through 0 (random) to +1 (perfect clustering). A value near +1 means areas with similar values are near each other — high values cluster near high values, and low near low.

name.text: M1Q04: You need to calculate the area of Indian states in square kilometers. Which CRS

questiontext.text: You need to calculate the area of Indian states in square kilometers. Which CRS type should you use?

generalfeedback.text: Area and distance calculations require a projected CRS that uses linear units (meters, kilometers). EPSG:4326 uses degrees, which would give area in square degrees — meaningless for real-world measurements.

answer.text: A projected CRS such as UTM Zone 43N (EPSG:32643)

feedback.text: Correct! Area and distance calculations require a projected CRS that uses linear units (meters, kilometers). EPSG:4326 uses degrees, which would give area in square degrees — meaningless for real-world measurements.

answer.text: A geodetic CRS such as WGS 84 (EPSG:4326)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Area and distance calculations require a projected CRS that uses linear units (meters, kilometers). EPSG:4326 uses degrees, which would give area in square degrees — meaningless for real-world measurements.

answer.text: Any CRS will give the same area result

feedback.text: Incorrect. Area and distance calculations require a projected CRS that uses linear units (meters, kilometers). EPSG:4326 uses degrees, which would give area in square degrees — meaningless for real-world measurements.

answer.text: No CRS is needed for area calculations

feedback.text: Incorrect. Area and distance calculations require a projected CRS that uses linear units (meters, kilometers). EPSG:4326 uses degrees, which would give area in square degrees — meaningless for real-world measurements.

generalfeedback.text: WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the most widely used geodetic CRS. It defines positions on the Earth’s surface using latitude and longitude in degrees. It’s used by GPS and is the default for most spatial data exchange.

answer.text: Geodetic (geographic) coordinate reference system using latitude/longitude in degrees

feedback.text: Correct! WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the most widely used geodetic CRS. It defines positions on the Earth’s surface using latitude and longitude in degrees. It’s used by GPS and is the default for most spatial data exchange.

answer.text: Projected coordinate reference system using meters

feedback.text: Incorrect. WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the most widely used geodetic CRS. It defines positions on the Earth’s surface using latitude and longitude in degrees. It’s used by GPS and is the default for most spatial data exchange.

answer.text: Local coordinate system specific to India

feedback.text: Incorrect. WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the most widely used geodetic CRS. It defines positions on the Earth’s surface using latitude and longitude in degrees. It’s used by GPS and is the default for most spatial data exchange.

answer.text: Raster-specific coordinate system for satellite imagery

feedback.text: Incorrect. WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the most widely used geodetic CRS. It defines positions on the Earth’s surface using latitude and longitude in degrees. It’s used by GPS and is the default for most spatial data exchange.

generalfeedback.text: No flat map can preserve all properties simultaneously. The Mercator projection is conformal (preserves angles/shapes locally) but greatly distorts areas, especially near the poles. Greenland appears as large as Africa, though Africa is 14 times larger.

answer.text: Angular relationships (shape), but distorts area

feedback.text: Correct! No flat map can preserve all properties simultaneously. The Mercator projection is conformal (preserves angles/shapes locally) but greatly distorts areas, especially near the poles. Greenland appears as large as Africa, though Africa is 14 times larger.

answer.text: Area, but distorts shape

feedback.text: Incorrect. No flat map can preserve all properties simultaneously. The Mercator projection is conformal (preserves angles/shapes locally) but greatly distorts areas, especially near the poles. Greenland appears as large as Africa, though Africa is 14 times larger.

answer.text: Distance from any point to any other point

feedback.text: Incorrect. No flat map can preserve all properties simultaneously. The Mercator projection is conformal (preserves angles/shapes locally) but greatly distorts areas, especially near the poles. Greenland appears as large as Africa, though Africa is 14 times larger.

answer.text: Both area and shape simultaneously

feedback.text: Incorrect. No flat map can preserve all properties simultaneously. The Mercator projection is conformal (preserves angles/shapes locally) but greatly distorts areas, especially near the poles. Greenland appears as large as Africa, though Africa is 14 times larger.

name.text: M1Q07: In the context of healthcare data, which is a limitation of traditional health d

questiontext.text: In the context of healthcare data, which is a limitation of traditional health data sources (census, longitudinal surveys)?

generalfeedback.text: Traditional health data (census, NFHS, DLHS) are carefully designed and high quality, but they are expensive, often aggregated to protect privacy (coarse resolution), and the more detailed they are, the less frequently they are collected.

answer.text: They are massive enterprises that are costly, coarse in resolution, and slow to update

feedback.text: Correct! Traditional health data (census, NFHS, DLHS) are carefully designed and high quality, but they are expensive, often aggregated to protect privacy (coarse resolution), and the more detailed they are, the less frequently they are collected.

answer.text: They are too detailed and updated too frequently

feedback.text: Incorrect. Traditional health data (census, NFHS, DLHS) are carefully designed and high quality, but they are expensive, often aggregated to protect privacy (coarse resolution), and the more detailed they are, the less frequently they are collected.

answer.text: They cannot be geo-referenced to any location

feedback.text: Incorrect. Traditional health data (census, NFHS, DLHS) are carefully designed and high quality, but they are expensive, often aggregated to protect privacy (coarse resolution), and the more detailed they are, the less frequently they are collected.

answer.text: They only cover urban populations

feedback.text: Incorrect. Traditional health data (census, NFHS, DLHS) are carefully designed and high quality, but they are expensive, often aggregated to protect privacy (coarse resolution), and the more detailed they are, the less frequently they are collected.

name.text: M1Q08: According to the CDC’s cartographic guidelines, which of the following is an ess

questiontext.text: According to the CDC’s cartographic guidelines, which of the following is an essential map element?

generalfeedback.text: The CDC’s GeoSWG guidelines specify that public health maps should include: title, borders, north arrow/graticule, scale bar, inset maps (if needed), labels, legend, data sources, dates, and projection information.

answer.text: All of the following: title, legend, scale bar, north arrow, data source

feedback.text: Correct! The CDC’s GeoSWG guidelines specify that public health maps should include: title, borders, north arrow/graticule, scale bar, inset maps (if needed), labels, legend, data sources, dates, and projection information.

answer.text: Only a title and legend are required

feedback.text: Incorrect. The CDC’s GeoSWG guidelines specify that public health maps should include: title, borders, north arrow/graticule, scale bar, inset maps (if needed), labels, legend, data sources, dates, and projection information.

answer.text: Only the data visualization layer is necessary

feedback.text: Incorrect. The CDC’s GeoSWG guidelines specify that public health maps should include: title, borders, north arrow/graticule, scale bar, inset maps (if needed), labels, legend, data sources, dates, and projection information.

answer.text: Map elements are optional and depend on journal preference

feedback.text: Incorrect. The CDC’s GeoSWG guidelines specify that public health maps should include: title, borders, north arrow/graticule, scale bar, inset maps (if needed), labels, legend, data sources, dates, and projection information.

name.text: M1Q09: Spatial Data Science differs from general Data Science primarily because it:

questiontext.text: Spatial Data Science differs from general Data Science primarily because it:

generalfeedback.text: SDS moves beyond simply mapping where things happen to understanding the spatial processes that drive patterns. It draws on geography, statistics, and computer science to analyze spatially-referenced data.

answer.text: Focuses on understanding why phenomena happen WHERE they do, not just what happens

feedback.text: Correct! SDS moves beyond simply mapping where things happen to understanding the spatial processes that drive patterns. It draws on geography, statistics, and computer science to analyze spatially-referenced data.

answer.text: Only uses satellite imagery as its data source

feedback.text: Incorrect. SDS moves beyond simply mapping where things happen to understanding the spatial processes that drive patterns. It draws on geography, statistics, and computer science to analyze spatially-referenced data.

answer.text: Requires proprietary GIS software like ArcGIS

feedback.text: Incorrect. SDS moves beyond simply mapping where things happen to understanding the spatial processes that drive patterns. It draws on geography, statistics, and computer science to analyze spatially-referenced data.

answer.text: Cannot use machine learning methods

feedback.text: Incorrect. SDS moves beyond simply mapping where things happen to understanding the spatial processes that drive patterns. It draws on geography, statistics, and computer science to analyze spatially-referenced data.

name.text: M1Q10: Complete Spatial Randomness (CSR) means that the location of an event is indepen

questiontext.text: Complete Spatial Randomness (CSR) means that the location of an event is independent of the locations of all other events.

generalfeedback.text: CSR is the null hypothesis in spatial analysis. Under CSR, events are equally likely to occur at any location and the position of one event does not influence the position of others. Most public health phenomena violate CSR due to spatial dependence.

answer.text: True

feedback.text: Correct! CSR is the null hypothesis in spatial analysis. Under CSR, events are equally likely to occur at any location and the position of one event does not influence the position of others. Most public health phenomena violate CSR due to spatial dependence.

answer.text: False

feedback.text: Incorrect. CSR is the null hypothesis in spatial analysis. Under CSR, events are equally likely to occur at any location and the position of one event does not influence the position of others. Most public health phenomena violate CSR due to spatial dependence.

name.text: M1Q11: The statement “natural phenomena are not spatially distributed at random” is the

questiontext.text: The statement “natural phenomena are not spatially distributed at random” is the principle of:

generalfeedback.text: Spatial dependence means nearby locations influence each other and tend to possess similar attributes. Temperature, rainfall, population density, and disease rates all exhibit spatial dependence.

answer.text: Spatial dependence

feedback.text: Correct! Spatial dependence means nearby locations influence each other and tend to possess similar attributes. Temperature, rainfall, population density, and disease rates all exhibit spatial dependence.

answer.text: Spatial independence

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial dependence means nearby locations influence each other and tend to possess similar attributes. Temperature, rainfall, population density, and disease rates all exhibit spatial dependence.

answer.text: Geographic determinism

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial dependence means nearby locations influence each other and tend to possess similar attributes. Temperature, rainfall, population density, and disease rates all exhibit spatial dependence.

answer.text: Environmental fallacy

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial dependence means nearby locations influence each other and tend to possess similar attributes. Temperature, rainfall, population density, and disease rates all exhibit spatial dependence.

name.text: M1Q12: A researcher wants to share spatial data globally using a standard that works wi

questiontext.text: A researcher wants to share spatial data globally using a standard that works with GPS devices. Which CRS should they use?

generalfeedback.text: WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the global standard geodetic CRS used by GPS. It’s ideal for data sharing and storage. For analysis requiring distance/area calculations, transform to an appropriate projected CRS.

feedback.text: Correct! WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the global standard geodetic CRS used by GPS. It’s ideal for data sharing and storage. For analysis requiring distance/area calculations, transform to an appropriate projected CRS.

answer.text: UTM Zone 43N (EPSG:32643)

feedback.text: Incorrect. WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the global standard geodetic CRS used by GPS. It’s ideal for data sharing and storage. For analysis requiring distance/area calculations, transform to an appropriate projected CRS.

answer.text: Mollweide Equal Area projection

feedback.text: Incorrect. WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the global standard geodetic CRS used by GPS. It’s ideal for data sharing and storage. For analysis requiring distance/area calculations, transform to an appropriate projected CRS.

answer.text: India National CRS (Everest 1830)

feedback.text: Incorrect. WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the global standard geodetic CRS used by GPS. It’s ideal for data sharing and storage. For analysis requiring distance/area calculations, transform to an appropriate projected CRS.

name.text: M1Q13: Match each spatial autocorrelation index with its category:

questiontext.text:
Match each spatial autocorrelation index with its category:

Moran’s I → Global measure

Getis-Ord Gi* → Local measure (hot/cold spots)

Geary’s C → Global measure

Local Moran’s I (LISA) → Local measure (cluster types)

Global measures (Moran’s I, Geary’s C) provide a single summary statistic for the entire study area. Local measures (LISA, Getis-Ord) identify WHERE clusters occur, enabling targeted intervention.

generalfeedback.text: Global measures (Moran’s I, Geary’s C) provide a single summary statistic for the entire study area. Local measures (LISA, Getis-Ord) identify WHERE clusters occur, enabling targeted intervention.

name.text: M1Q14: Why is reproducibility particularly important in spatial data science for public

questiontext.text: Why is reproducibility particularly important in spatial data science for public health?

generalfeedback.text: Public health decisions (resource allocation, intervention targeting) based on spatial analysis must be transparent and verifiable. Using R with version-controlled scripts ensures any researcher can reproduce and validate the analysis.

answer.text: Because policy decisions based on spatial analysis must be transparent, verifiable, and repeatable by others

feedback.text: Correct! Public health decisions (resource allocation, intervention targeting) based on spatial analysis must be transparent and verifiable. Using R with version-controlled scripts ensures any researcher can reproduce and validate the analysis.

answer.text: Because GIS software is expensive and only some researchers have access

feedback.text: Incorrect. Public health decisions (resource allocation, intervention targeting) based on spatial analysis must be transparent and verifiable. Using R with version-controlled scripts ensures any researcher can reproduce and validate the analysis.

answer.text: Because spatial data never changes over time

feedback.text: Incorrect. Public health decisions (resource allocation, intervention targeting) based on spatial analysis must be transparent and verifiable. Using R with version-controlled scripts ensures any researcher can reproduce and validate the analysis.

answer.text: Because reproducibility is only required for clinical trials, not epidemiological studies

feedback.text: Incorrect. Public health decisions (resource allocation, intervention targeting) based on spatial analysis must be transparent and verifiable. Using R with version-controlled scripts ensures any researcher can reproduce and validate the analysis.

name.text: M1Q15: John Snow’s 1854 cholera map of London’s Broad Street is a landmark in:

questiontext.text: John Snow’s 1854 cholera map of London’s Broad Street is a landmark in:

generalfeedback.text: Snow’s map showed the clustering of cholera deaths around the Broad Street pump, providing compelling spatial evidence for waterborne transmission — decades before the cholera bacterium was identified. This is foundational spatial epidemiology.

answer.text: Spatial epidemiology — demonstrating how mapping disease cases can reveal transmission sources

feedback.text: Correct! Snow’s map showed the clustering of cholera deaths around the Broad Street pump, providing compelling spatial evidence for waterborne transmission — decades before the cholera bacterium was identified. This is foundational spatial epidemiology.

answer.text: Laboratory microbiology — identifying the cholera bacterium

feedback.text: Incorrect. Snow’s map showed the clustering of cholera deaths around the Broad Street pump, providing compelling spatial evidence for waterborne transmission — decades before the cholera bacterium was identified. This is foundational spatial epidemiology.

answer.text: Demographic transition theory

feedback.text: Incorrect. Snow’s map showed the clustering of cholera deaths around the Broad Street pump, providing compelling spatial evidence for waterborne transmission — decades before the cholera bacterium was identified. This is foundational spatial epidemiology.

answer.text: Development of the germ theory of disease

feedback.text: Incorrect. Snow’s map showed the clustering of cholera deaths around the Broad Street pump, providing compelling spatial evidence for waterborne transmission — decades before the cholera bacterium was identified. This is foundational spatial epidemiology.
